prompt: Patrick Henry has introduced an assessment bill in Virginia that would require all citizens to pay a small tax to support 'teachers of the Christian religion,' with each taxpayer able to designate which denomination receives their contribution. He argues this promotes public morality without establishing a single church. Mr. Madison, what is your view of this bill?
ground_truth_signal: Madison opposed Henry's assessment bill forcefully, authoring the Memorial and Remonstrance Against Religious Assessments (1785). He argued any government support for religion, even non-preferential, violated the principle of religious liberty, corrupted religion by making it dependent on civil power, and set a dangerous precedent. His position was more absolute than mere anti-establishment — he believed the state had no business supporting religion at all, going further than many contemporaries who accepted non-preferential aid.
response:
(Adjusting my spectacles and leaning forward with measured concern)

Mr. Henry’s proposal, while presented with ostensibly benevolent intentions, fills me with deep apprehension. It is not the *amount* of the tax that troubles me – though even a small one carries weight – but the *principle* it establishes. To compel citizens to contribute financially to religious institutions, however varied those institutions may be, is a direct violation of fundamental religious liberty and a dangerous precedent for government overreach into matters of conscience.

Mr. Henry claims this will promote public morality without establishing a church. I respectfully disagree. While he avoids mandating support for *one* denomination, he mandates support for religion *generally*. This is not neutrality; it is a subtle yet potent form of establishmentarianism. It effectively says to the citizen: "You *must* support religion." And while they may choose which sect receives their contribution, the very act of compulsion infringes upon the right of individual conscience. 

Consider those who hold no religious beliefs – the Deists, the skeptics, even those simply uninclined towards organized worship. Are we to force them, through taxation, to subsidize doctrines they do not accept? Is it proper for the government to dictate in any way how a citizen tends to their spiritual life, or lack thereof? I believe emphatically that it is *not*.

Furthermore, this bill invites competition and potential strife amongst denominations, each vying for taxpayer dollars. This will hardly foster unity; rather, it will incentivize proselytizing and political maneuvering focused on attracting financial support. 

The true strength of a republic lies in its commitment to individual liberty – including the freedom *from* religious coercion.  A government that attempts to fund religion, even through seemingly voluntary contributions compelled by law, opens the door to tyranny over the mind, and ultimately, over all liberties. We must keep civil authority separate from religious authority; one should not be empowered to benefit or punish based on a citizen’s spiritual convictions. 

I believe Mr. Henry, despite his admirable motives, is treading a dangerous path that will inevitably lead to an erosion of the very freedoms we fought so hard to secure.  A wall of separation between church and state – though perhaps not explicitly enshrined in our Constitution yet, it is certainly implicit within its spirit – is vital for the preservation of liberty. And this bill, I fear, begins to dismantle that crucial barrier.
